is a [video](/wiki/Video "Video") that integrates a [song](/wiki/Song "Song") or an [album](/wiki/Album "Album") with imagery that is produced for [promotional](/wiki/Promotion_(marketing) "Promotion (marketing)") or musical [artistic](/wiki/Artistic "Artistic") purposes. Modern music videos are primarily made and used as a music [marketing](/wiki/Marketing "Marketing") device intended to promote the sale of [music recordings](/wiki/Music_recording "Music recording"). These videos are typically shown on [music television](/wiki/Music_television "Music television") and on streaming video sites like [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"), or more rarely shown theatrically. They can be commercially issued on home video, either as video albums or video singles. The format has been described by various terms including "[illustrated song](/wiki/Illustrated_song "Illustrated song")", "filmed insert", "promotional (promo) film", "promotional clip", "promotional video", "song video", "song clip", "film clip", "video clip", or simply "video".

A **music video** is a [video](/wiki/Video "Video") that integrates a [song](/wiki/Song "Song") or an [album](/wiki/Album "Album") with imagery that is produced for [promotional](/wiki/Promotion_(marketing) "Promotion (marketing)") or musical [artistic](/wiki/Artistic "Artistic") purposes. Modern music videos are primarily made and used as a music [marketing](/wiki/Marketing "Marketing") device intended to promote the sale of [music recordings](/wiki/Music_recording "Music recording"). These videos are typically shown on [music television](/wiki/Music_television "Music television") and on streaming video sites like [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"), or more rarely shown theatrically. They can be commercially issued on home video, either as video albums or video singles. The format has been described by various terms including "[illustrated song](/wiki/Illustrated_song "Illustrated song")", "filmed insert", "promotional (promo) film", "promotional clip", "promotional video", "song video", "song clip", "film clip", "video clip", or simply "video".

While [musical short films](/wiki/Musical_short "Musical short") were popular as soon as recorded sound was introduced to theatrical film screenings in the 1920s, promotional music videos started becoming popular into the 1960s and the music video rose to prominence in the 1980s when American TV channel [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") based its format around the medium.

Music videos use a wide range of styles and contemporary video-making techniques, including [animation](/wiki/Animation "Animation"), [live-action](/wiki/Live_action "Live action"), [documentary](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film"), and non-narrative approaches such as [abstract film](/wiki/Non-narrative_film "Non-narrative film"). Combining these styles and techniques has become more popular due to the variety for the audience. Many music videos interpret images and scenes from the song's [lyrics](/wiki/Lyrics "Lyrics"), while others take a more thematic approach. Other music videos may not have any concept, being only a filmed version of the song's live [concert](/wiki/Concert "Concert") performance.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

In 1894, [sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music") publishers [Edward B. Marks](/wiki/Edward_B._Marks "Edward B. Marks") and Joe Stern hired electrician George Thomas and various artists to promote sales of their song "[The Little Lost Child](/wiki/The_Little_Lost_Child "The Little Lost Child")".[[2]](#cite_note-music_video_1900_style_on_PBS_Kids_Go!-2) Using a [magic lantern](/wiki/Magic_lantern "Magic lantern"), Thomas projected a series of still images on a screen simultaneous to live performances. This would become a popular form of entertainment known as the [illustrated song](/wiki/Illustrated_song "Illustrated song"), the first step toward music video.[[2]](#cite_note-music_video_1900_style_on_PBS_Kids_Go!-2)

### Talkies, soundies and shorts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Talkies, soundies and shorts")]

With the arrival of "[talkies](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film")" many [musical short films](/wiki/Musical_short "Musical short") were produced. [Vitaphone](/wiki/Vitaphone "Vitaphone") shorts (produced by [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.")) featured many bands, vocalists, and dancers. Animation artist [Max Fleischer](/wiki/Max_Fleischer "Max Fleischer") introduced a series of sing-along short cartoons called *[Screen Songs](/wiki/Screen_Songs "Screen Songs")*, which invited audiences to sing along to popular songs by "following the bouncing ball", which is similar to a modern karaoke machine. Early cartoons featured popular musicians performing their hit songs on camera in live-action segments during the [cartoons](/wiki/Cartoon "Cartoon"). [John Logie Baird](/wiki/John_Logie_Baird "John Logie Baird") created [Phonovision discs](/wiki/Phonovision "Phonovision") featuring [Betty Bolton](/wiki/Betty_Bolton "Betty Bolton") and other singers from the 1930s. The early animated films by [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney"), such as the *[Silly Symphonies](/wiki/Silly_Symphony "Silly Symphony")* shorts and especially *[Fantasia](/wiki/Fantasia_(1940_film) "Fantasia (1940 film)")*, which featured several interpretations of classical pieces, were built around music. The [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") cartoons, even today billed as *[Looney Tunes](/wiki/Looney_Tunes "Looney Tunes")* and *[Merrie Melodies](/wiki/Merrie_Melodies "Merrie Melodies")*, were initially fashioned around specific songs from upcoming Warner Bros. [musical films](/wiki/Musical_film "Musical film"). Live-action musical shorts, featuring such popular artists as [Cab Calloway](/wiki/Cab_Calloway "Cab Calloway"), were also distributed to theaters.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[Blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") singer [Bessie Smith](/wiki/Bessie_Smith "Bessie Smith") appeared in a two-reel short film called *[St. Louis Blues](/wiki/St._Louis_Blues_(1929_film) "St. Louis Blues (1929 film)")* featuring a dramatized performance of the hit song. Numerous other musicians appeared in short musical subjects during this period.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

*[Soundies](/wiki/Soundies "Soundies")*, produced and released for the [Panoram](/wiki/Panoram "Panoram") film jukebox, were musical films that often included short dance sequences, similar to later music videos.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Musician [Louis Jordan](/wiki/Louis_Jordan "Louis Jordan") made short films for his songs, some of which were spliced together into a feature film, *Lookout Sister*. These films were, according to music historian [Donald Clarke](/wiki/Donald_Clarke_(writer) "Donald Clarke (writer)"), the "ancestors" of music video.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Gentlemen_Prefer_Blondes_Movie_Trailer_Screenshot_%2836%29.jpg/250px-Gentlemen_Prefer_Blondes_Movie_Trailer_Screenshot_%2836%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gentlemen_Prefer_Blondes_Movie_Trailer_Screenshot_(36).jpg)

Musicals of the 1950s led to short-form music videos.

[Musical films](/wiki/Musical_film "Musical film") were another important precursor to a music video, and several well-known music videos have imitated the style of classic Hollywood musicals from the 1930s–50s. One of the best-known examples is [Madonna's](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna") 1985 video for "[Material Girl](/wiki/Material_Girl "Material Girl")" (directed by [Mary Lambert](/wiki/Mary_Lambert_(director) "Mary Lambert (director)"))[[7]](#cite_note-7) which was closely modelled on [Jack Cole](/wiki/Jack_Cole_(choreographer) "Jack Cole (choreographer)")'s staging of "[Diamonds Are a Girl's Best Friend](/wiki/Diamonds_Are_a_Girl%27s_Best_Friend "Diamonds Are a Girl's Best Friend")" from the film *[Gentlemen Prefer Blondes](/wiki/Gentlemen_Prefer_Blondes_(1953_film) "Gentlemen Prefer Blondes (1953 film)")*. Several of [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson")'s videos show the unmistakable influence of the dance sequences in classic Hollywood musicals, including the landmark "[Thriller](/wiki/Michael_Jackson%27s_Thriller_(music_video) "Michael Jackson's Thriller (music video)")" and the [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese")–directed "[Bad](/wiki/Bad_(Michael_Jackson_song) "Bad (Michael Jackson song)")", which was influenced by the stylized dance "fights" in the film version of *[West Side Story](/wiki/West_Side_Story_(1961_film) "West Side Story (1961 film)")*.[[8]](#cite_note-8) According to the Internet Accuracy Project, [DJ](/wiki/Disc_jockey "Disc jockey")/singer J. P. "[The Big Bopper](/wiki/The_Big_Bopper "The Big Bopper")" Richardson was the first to coin the phrase "music video", in 1959.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In his autobiography, Tony Bennett claims to have created "...the first music video" when he was filmed walking along the [Serpentine](/wiki/Serpentine_(lake) "Serpentine (lake)") in [Hyde Park, London](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_London "Hyde Park, London"), with the resulting clip being set to his recording of the song "[Stranger in Paradise](/wiki/Stranger_in_Paradise_(song) "Stranger in Paradise (song)")".[[10]](#cite_note-10) The clip was sent to UK and US television stations and aired on shows including [Dick Clark](/wiki/Dick_Clark "Dick Clark")'s *[American Bandstand](/wiki/American_Bandstand "American Bandstand")*.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
The oldest example of a promotional music video with similarities to more abstract, modern videos seems to be the [Czechoslovak](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") "Dáme si do bytu" ("We´ll put in the apartment") created and directed by [Ladislav Rychman](/wiki/Ladislav_Rychman "Ladislav Rychman").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Beginnings of popular music television and promotional clips: late 1950s–1973

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Beginnings of popular music television and promotional clips: late 1950s–1973")]

In the late 1950s[[14]](#cite_note-Almind-14) the [Scopitone](/wiki/Scopitone "Scopitone"), a visual jukebox, was introduced in France and short films were produced by many French artists, such as [Serge Gainsbourg](/wiki/Serge_Gainsbourg "Serge Gainsbourg"), [Françoise Hardy](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7oise_Hardy "Françoise Hardy"), [Jacques Dutronc](/wiki/Jacques_Dutronc "Jacques Dutronc"), and the Belgian [Jacques Brel](/wiki/Jacques_Brel "Jacques Brel") to accompany their songs. Its use spread to other countries, and similar machines such as the [Cinebox](/wiki/Cinebox "Cinebox") in Italy and [Color-sonic](/wiki/Color-sonic "Color-sonic") in the U.S. were patented.[[14]](#cite_note-Almind-14) In 1961, for the Canadian-produced show *[Singalong Jubilee](/wiki/Singalong_Jubilee "Singalong Jubilee")*, Manny Pittson began pre-recording the music audio, went on-location and taped various visuals with the musicians lip-synching, then edited the audio and video together. Most music numbers were taped in-studio on stage, and the location shoot "videos" were to add variety.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In 1964, [Kenneth Anger](/wiki/Kenneth_Anger "Kenneth Anger")'s [experimental](/wiki/Experimental_film "Experimental film") short film, *[Scorpio Rising](/wiki/Scorpio_Rising_(film) "Scorpio Rising (film)")* used popular songs instead of dialogue.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

On 1 January 1964, [Johnnie Stewart](/wiki/Johnnie_Stewart "Johnnie Stewart") and [Stanley Dorfman](/wiki/Stanley_Dorfman "Stanley Dorfman") created the British [chart](/wiki/Record_chart "Record chart") music television series *[Top of the Pops](/wiki/Top_of_the_Pops "Top of the Pops"),* which they produced in tandem and directed in weekly rotation until the 1970s*.[[17]](#cite_note-Humphries-2013-17)* The show's format created a demand for frequent studio appearances by renowned British and US artists at short notice, as the charts came out on Tuesday mornings and the show was taped live on Thursdays. Coupled with the artists busy touring schedules and subsequent requests from broadcasters in Europe and America to showcase popular British acts, ultimately prompted the production of pre-recorded or filmed inserts referred to as "promotional videos." These videos served as substitutes for live performances by the artists and played a pivotal role in the development of the music video genre.[[18]](#cite_note-Humphries-2013a-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Riefe-2016a-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Reed-2002a-20) During the early stages of the show's introduction in 1964, when alternative footage was unavailable, Dorfman and Stewart resorted to capturing footage of the enthusiastic audience dancing. However, a significant change took place in October 1964 when a decision was made to occasionally introduce a [dance troupe](/wiki/Dance_troupe "Dance troupe") with choreographed routines for specific tracks. This addition brought a new dynamic to the show, enhancing its visual appeal and diversifying the entertainment value for viewers.[[21]](#cite_note-21) One notable example was the video for [Roy Orbison](/wiki/Roy_Orbison "Roy Orbison")'s song '[Oh Pretty Woman](/wiki/Oh,_Pretty_Woman "Oh, Pretty Woman")', which Dorfman filmed and directed in the rooftop garden of London's Kensington-based [Derry and Toms](/wiki/Derry_%26_Toms "Derry & Toms") department store on 19 October 1964 as a visual accompaniment to the song. It subsequently aired on Top of the Pops on 22 October, 29, as well as 12 November and 19."[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) By the 1970s, Top of the Pops had an average weekly viewership of 12,500,000 people, had solidified its status as the premier international platform for artists launching new records at the time,[[24]](#cite_note-Hull_Daily_Mail-1970-24) had firmly established the significance of promotional film clips as a crucial tool for promoting the careers of emerging artists and generating buzz for new releases by established acts, and was significant in developing and popularizing what would later become the music video genre across the globe.[[19]](#cite_note-Riefe-2016a-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Reed-2002a-20)

In 1964, [The Moody Blues](/wiki/The_Moody_Blues "The Moody Blues") producer [Alex Murray](/wiki/Alex_Wharton "Alex Wharton") wanted to promote his version of "[Go Now](/wiki/Go_Now "Go Now")". The short film clip he produced and directed to promote the single has a striking visual style that predates [Queen's](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)") similar "[Bohemian Rhapsody](/wiki/Bohemian_Rhapsody "Bohemian Rhapsody")" video by a full decade[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. It also predates what [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") did with promotional films of their single "[Paperback Writer](/wiki/Paperback_Writer "Paperback Writer")" and B-Side "[Rain](/wiki/Rain_(The_Beatles_song) "Rain (The Beatles song)")", both released in 1966.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Also in 1964, the Beatles starred in their first feature film, *[A Hard Day's Night](/wiki/A_Hard_Day%27s_Night_(film) "A Hard Day's Night (film)")*, directed by US filmmaker [Richard Lester](/wiki/Richard_Lester "Richard Lester"). Shot in [black-and-white](/wiki/Black-and-white "Black-and-white") and presented as a [mock documentary](/wiki/Mockumentary "Mockumentary"), it interspersed comedic and dialogue sequences with musical tones. The musical sequences furnished basic templates on which numerous subsequent music videos were modeled. It was the direct model for the successful American TV series *[The Monkees](/wiki/The_Monkees_(TV_series) "The Monkees (TV series)")* (1966–1968), which was similarly composed of film segments that were created to accompany various Monkees songs.[[25]](#cite_note-TMT-25) The Beatles' second feature, *[Help!](/wiki/Help!_(film) "Help! (film)")* (1965), was a much more lavish affair, filmed in color in London and on international locations. The title track sequence, filmed in black-and-white, is arguably one of the prime archetypes of the modern performance-style music video, employing rhythmic cross-cutting, contrasting long shots and close-ups, and infrequent shots and camera angles, such as the shot 50 seconds into the song, in which [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison")'s left hand and the neck of his guitar are seen in sharp focus in the foreground while the completely out-of-focus figure of [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon") sings in the background.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1965, the Beatles started making promotional clips (then known as "filmed inserts") for distribution and broadcast on Top of the Pops and in different countries—primarily the [U.S.](/wiki/United_States "United States")—so they could promote their record releases without having to make in-person appearances. Their first batch of promo films shot in late 1965 (including their then-current single, "[Day Tripper](/wiki/Day_Tripper "Day Tripper")"/"[We Can Work It Out](/wiki/We_Can_Work_It_Out "We Can Work It Out")"), were fairly straightforward mimed-in-studio performance pieces (albeit sometimes in silly sets) and meant to blend in fairly seamlessly with television shows like *Top of the Pops* and *[Hullabaloo](/wiki/Hullabaloo_(TV_series) "Hullabaloo (TV series)")*. By the time the Beatles stopped touring in late 1966, their promotional films, like their recordings, had become highly sophisticated. In May 1966, they filmed two sets of colour promotional clips for their current single "[Rain](/wiki/Rain_(The_Beatles_song) "Rain (The Beatles song)")"/"[Paperback Writer](/wiki/Paperback_Writer "Paperback Writer")" all directed by [Michael Lindsay-Hogg](/wiki/Michael_Lindsay-Hogg "Michael Lindsay-Hogg"),[[26]](#cite_note-26) who went on to direct *[The Rolling Stones Rock and Roll Circus](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones_Rock_and_Roll_Circus "The Rolling Stones Rock and Roll Circus")* and the Beatles' final film, *[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(1970_film) "Let It Be (1970 film)")*. It aired on Top of the Pops on 2 June.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The colour promotional clips for "[Strawberry Fields Forever](/wiki/Strawberry_Fields_Forever#Promotional_film "Strawberry Fields Forever")" and "[Penny Lane](/wiki/Penny_Lane "Penny Lane")", made in early 1967 and directed by Peter Goldman,[[28]](#cite_note-28) took the promotional film format to a new level. They used techniques borrowed from underground and avant-garde film, including reversed film and slow motion, dramatic lighting, unusual camera angles, and colour filtering added in post-production. At the end of 1967 the group released their third film, the one hour, made-for-television project *[Magical Mystery Tour](/wiki/Magical_Mystery_Tour_(film) "Magical Mystery Tour (film)")*; it was written and directed by the group and first broadcast on the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") on [Boxing Day](/wiki/Boxing_Day "Boxing Day") 1967. Although poorly received at the time for lacking a narrative structure, it showed the group to be adventurous music filmmakers in their own right.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/TrailerUSHelp.jpg/250px-TrailerUSHelp.jpg)](/wiki/File:TrailerUSHelp.jpg)

The Beatles in *Help!*

[Concert films](/wiki/Concert_film "Concert film") were being released in the mid-1960s, at least as early as 1964, with the *[T.A.M.I. Show](/wiki/T.A.M.I._Show "T.A.M.I. Show")*.

The monochrome 1965 clip for [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan")'s "[Subterranean Homesick Blues](/wiki/Subterranean_Homesick_Blues "Subterranean Homesick Blues")" filmed by [D. A. Pennebaker](/wiki/D._A._Pennebaker "D. A. Pennebaker") was featured in Pennebaker's Dylan film documentary *[Dont Look Back](/wiki/Dont_Look_Back "Dont Look Back")*. Eschewing any attempt to simulate performance or present a narrative, the clip shows Dylan standing in a city back alley, silently shuffling a series of large cue cards (bearing key words from the song's lyrics).

Besides the Beatles, many other British artists made "filmed inserts" so they could be screened on TV when the bands were not available to appear live. [The Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who") featured in several promotional clips, beginning with their 1965 clip for "[I Can't Explain](/wiki/I_Can%27t_Explain "I Can't Explain")". Their plot clip for "[Happy Jack](/wiki/Happy_Jack_(song) "Happy Jack (song)")" (1966) shows the band acting like a gang of thieves. The promo film to "[Call Me Lightning](/wiki/Call_Me_Lightning_(song) "Call Me Lightning (song)")" (1968) tells a story of how drummer [Keith Moon](/wiki/Keith_Moon "Keith Moon") came to join the group: The other three band members are having tea inside what looks like an abandoned hangar when suddenly a "bleeding box" arrives, out of which jumps a fast-running, time lapse, Moon that the other members subsequently try to get a hold of in a sped-up slapstick chasing sequence to wind him down. [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd") produced promotional films for their songs, including "[San Francisco: Film](/wiki/San_Francisco:_Film "San Francisco: Film")", directed by [Anthony Stern](/wiki/Anthony_Stern "Anthony Stern"), "[Scarecrow](/wiki/The_Scarecrow_(Pink_Floyd_song) "The Scarecrow (Pink Floyd song)")", "[Arnold Layne](/wiki/Arnold_Layne "Arnold Layne")" and "[Interstellar Overdrive](/wiki/Interstellar_Overdrive "Interstellar Overdrive")", the latter directed by [Peter Whitehead](/wiki/Peter_Whitehead_(filmmaker) "Peter Whitehead (filmmaker)"), who also made several pioneering clips for [The Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") between 1966 and 1968. [The Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks") made one of the first "[plot](/wiki/Plot_(narrative) "Plot (narrative)")" promotional clips for a song. For their single "[Dead End Street](/wiki/Dead_End_Street_(song) "Dead End Street (song)")" (1966) a miniature comic movie was made. The BBC reportedly refused to air the clip because it was considered to be in "poor taste".[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The Rolling Stones appeared in many promotional clips for their songs in the 1960s. In 1966, [Peter Whitehead](/wiki/Peter_Whitehead_(filmmaker) "Peter Whitehead (filmmaker)") directed two promo clips for their single "[Have You Seen Your Mother, Baby, Standing In The Shadow?](/wiki/Have_You_Seen_Your_Mother,_Baby,_Standing_In_The_Shadow%3F "Have You Seen Your Mother, Baby, Standing In The Shadow?")"[[30]](#cite_note-mvdbase.com-30) In 1967, Whitehead directed a plot clip colour promo clip for the Stones single "[We Love You](/wiki/We_Love_You "We Love You")", which first aired in August 1967.[[31]](#cite_note-31) This clip featured sped-up footage of the group recording in the studio, intercut with a mock trial that clearly alludes to the drug prosecutions of [Mick Jagger](/wiki/Mick_Jagger "Mick Jagger") and [Keith Richards](/wiki/Keith_Richards "Keith Richards") underway at that time. Jagger's girlfriend [Marianne Faithfull](/wiki/Marianne_Faithfull "Marianne Faithfull") appears in the trial scenes and presents the "judge" (Richards) with what may be the infamous fur rug that had featured so prominently in the press reports of the drug bust at Richards' house in early 1967. When it is pulled back, it reveals an apparently naked Jagger with chains around his ankles. The clip concludes with scenes of the Stones in the studio intercut with footage that had previously been used in the "concert version" promo clip for "Have You Seen Your Mother, Baby". The group also filmed a color promo clip for the song "[2000 Light Years from Home](/wiki/2000_Light_Years_from_Home "2000 Light Years from Home")" (from their album *[Their Satanic Majesties Request](/wiki/Their_Satanic_Majesties_Request "Their Satanic Majesties Request")*) directed by [Michael Lindsay-Hogg](/wiki/Michael_Lindsay-Hogg "Michael Lindsay-Hogg").[[30]](#cite_note-mvdbase.com-30) In 1968, Michael Lindsay-Hogg directed three clips for their single "[Jumpin' Jack Flash](/wiki/Jumpin%27_Jack_Flash "Jumpin' Jack Flash")" / "Child of the Moon"—a color clip for "Child of the Moon" and two different clips for "Jumpin' Jack Flash". In 1968, they collaborated with [Jean-Luc Godard](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Godard "Jean-Luc Godard") on the film *[Sympathy for the Devil](/wiki/Sympathy_for_the_Devil_(1968_film) "Sympathy for the Devil (1968 film)")*, which mixed Godard's politics with documentary footage of the song's evolution during recording sessions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1966, [Nancy Sinatra](/wiki/Nancy_Sinatra "Nancy Sinatra") filmed a clip for her song "[These Boots Are Made for Walkin'](/wiki/These_Boots_Are_Made_for_Walkin%27 "These Boots Are Made for Walkin'")". [Roy Orbison](/wiki/Roy_Orbison "Roy Orbison") appeared in promotional clips, such as his 1968 hit, "Walk On".[[32]](#cite_note-32)

During late 1972–73, [Alice Cooper](/wiki/Alice_Cooper "Alice Cooper") featured in a series of promotional films: "[Elected](/wiki/Elected_(song) "Elected (song)")", "[Hello Hooray](/wiki/Hello_Hooray "Hello Hooray")", "No More Mr. Nice Guy" and "[Teenage Lament '74](/wiki/Teenage_Lament_%2774 "Teenage Lament '74")". Also during late 1972–73, [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie") featured in a series of promotional films directed by pop photographer [Mick Rock](/wiki/Mick_Rock "Mick Rock"), who worked extensively with Bowie in this period. Rock directed and edited four clips to promote four consecutive David Bowie singles—"[John, I'm Only Dancing](/wiki/John,_I%27m_Only_Dancing "John, I'm Only Dancing")" (May 1972), "[The Jean Genie](/wiki/The_Jean_Genie "The Jean Genie")" (November 1972), the December 1972 US re-release of "[Space Oddity](/wiki/Space_Oddity "Space Oddity")" and the 1973 release of the single "[Life on Mars?](/wiki/Life_on_Mars%3F "Life on Mars?")" (lifted from Bowie's earlier album *[Hunky Dory](/wiki/Hunky_Dory "Hunky Dory")*). The clip for "John, I'm Only Dancing" was made with a budget of just [US$](/wiki/US$ "US$")200 and filmed at the afternoon rehearsal for Bowie's [Rainbow Theatre](/wiki/Rainbow_Theatre "Rainbow Theatre") concert on August 19, 1972. It shows Bowie and band mimicking to the record intercut with footage of the [Lindsay Kemp](/wiki/Lindsay_Kemp "Lindsay Kemp") mime troupe, dancing on stage and behind a back-lit screen. The clip was turned down by the BBC, who reportedly found the homosexual overtones of the film distasteful; accordingly, *Top of the Pops* replaced it with footage of bikers and a dancer.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The "Jean Genie" clip, produced for just US$350, was shot in one day and edited in less than two days. It intercuts footage of Bowie and band in concert with contrasting footage of the group in a photographic studio, wearing black stage outfits, and standing against a white background. It also includes location footage with Bowie and [Cyrinda Foxe](/wiki/Cyrinda_Foxe "Cyrinda Foxe") (a MainMan employee and a friend of David and [Angie Bowie](/wiki/Angie_Bowie "Angie Bowie")) shot in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") outside the famous [Mars Hotel](/wiki/Mars_Hotel "Mars Hotel"), with Fox posing provocatively in the street while Bowie lounges against the wall, smoking.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Country music also picked up on the trend of promotional film clips to publicize songs. Sam Lovullo, the producer of the television series *[Hee Haw](/wiki/Hee_Haw "Hee Haw")*, explained his show presented "what were, in reality, the first musical videos",[[35]](#cite_note-Lovullo-35) while JMI Records made the same claim with [Don Williams](/wiki/Don_Williams "Don Williams")' 1973 song "[The Shelter of Your Eyes](/wiki/The_Shelter_of_Your_Eyes "The Shelter of Your Eyes")".[[36]](#cite_note-Millard-36) Country music historian Bob Millard wrote that JMI had pioneered the country music video concept by "producing a 3-minute film" to go along with Williams' song.[[36]](#cite_note-Millard-36) Lovullo said his videos were conceptualized by having the show's staff go to nearby rural areas and film animals and farmers, before editing the footage to fit the storyline of a particular song. "The video material was a very workable production item for the show," he wrote. "It provided picture stories for songs. However, some of our guests felt the videos took attention away from their live performances, which they hoped would promote record sales. If they had a hit song, they didn't want to play it under comic barnyard footage." The concept's mixed reaction eventually spelled an end to the "video" concept on *Hee Haw*.[[35]](#cite_note-Lovullo-35) Promotional films of country music songs, however, continued to be produced.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1974, the band [Sparks](/wiki/Sparks_(band) "Sparks (band)") made a promotional video for their song "[This Town Ain't Big Enough for Both of Us](/wiki/This_Town_Ain%27t_Big_Enough_for_Both_of_Us "This Town Ain't Big Enough for Both of Us")".[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### 1974–1980

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1974–1980")]

The Australian TV shows *[Countdown](/wiki/Countdown_(Australian_TV_series) "Countdown (Australian TV series)")* and *[Sounds](/wiki/Sounds_(Australian_TV_series) "Sounds (Australian TV series)")*, both of which premiered in 1974, followed in the steps of the UK's *Top of the Pops* and were significant in developing and popularizing what would later become the music video genre in Australia and other countries, and in establishing the importance of promotional film clips as a means of promoting both emerging acts and new releases by established acts. In early 1974, former radio DJ [Graham Webb](/wiki/Graham_Webb_(Australian_broadcaster) "Graham Webb (Australian broadcaster)") launched a weekly teen-oriented TV music show which screened on [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney")'s [ATN-7](/wiki/ATN-7 "ATN-7") on Saturday mornings; this was renamed *Sounds Unlimited* in 1975 and later shortened simply to *Sounds*. In need of material for the show, Webb approached Seven newsroom staffer [Russell Mulcahy](/wiki/Russell_Mulcahy "Russell Mulcahy") and asked him to shoot film footage to accompany popular songs for which there were no purpose-made clips (e.g. [Harry Nilsson](/wiki/Harry_Nilsson "Harry Nilsson")'s "[Everybody's Talkin](/wiki/Everybody%27s_Talkin "Everybody's Talkin")"). Using this method, Webb and Mulcahy assembled a collection of about 25 clips for the show. The success of his early efforts encouraged Mulcahy to quit his TV job and become a full-time director, and he made clips for several popular Australian acts including [Stylus](/wiki/Stylus_(band) "Stylus (band)"), [Marcia Hines](/wiki/Marcia_Hines "Marcia Hines"), [Hush](/wiki/Hush_(band) "Hush (band)") and [AC/DC](/wiki/AC/DC "AC/DC").[[38]](#cite_note-smh.com.au-38) As it gained popularity, *Countdown* talent coordinator [Molly Meldrum](/wiki/Molly_Meldrum "Molly Meldrum") and producer Michael Shrimpton quickly realized that "film clips" were becoming an important new commodity in music marketing. Despite the show's minuscule budget, *Countdown*'s original director [Paul Drane](/wiki/Paul_Drane "Paul Drane") was able to create several memorable music videos especially for the show, including the classic film-clips for the AC/DC hits "[It's a Long Way to the Top (If You Wanna Rock 'n' Roll)](/wiki/It%27s_a_Long_Way_to_the_Top_(If_You_Wanna_Rock_%27n%27_Roll) "It's a Long Way to the Top (If You Wanna Rock 'n' Roll)")" and "[Jailbreak](/wiki/Jailbreak_(AC/DC_song) "Jailbreak (AC/DC song)")".[[38]](#cite_note-smh.com.au-38) After relocating to the [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") in the mid-1970s, Mulcahy made successful promo films for several noted British pop acts—his early UK credits included [XTC](/wiki/XTC_(band) "XTC (band)")'s "[Making Plans for Nigel](/wiki/Making_Plans_for_Nigel "Making Plans for Nigel")" (1979) and his landmark video clip for [The Buggles](/wiki/The_Buggles "The Buggles")' "[Video Killed the Radio Star](/wiki/Video_Killed_the_Radio_Star "Video Killed the Radio Star")" (1979), which became the first music video played on [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") in 1981.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Queen_%40_United_Center%2C_Chicago_6-19-2014_%2814486803126%29.jpg/250px-Queen_%40_United_Center%2C_Chicago_6-19-2014_%2814486803126%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_@_United_Center,_Chicago_6-19-2014_(14486803126).jpg)

Footage of [Freddie Mercury](/wiki/Freddie_Mercury "Freddie Mercury") in the ["Bohemian Rhapsody" music video](/wiki/Bohemian_Rhapsody#Promotional_video "Bohemian Rhapsody") during a Queen + Adam Lambert concert at the [United Center](/wiki/United_Center "United Center"), Chicago

In 1975, [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)") employed [Bruce Gowers](/wiki/Bruce_Gowers "Bruce Gowers") to make a promotional [video](/wiki/Video "Video") to show their new single "[Bohemian Rhapsody](/wiki/Bohemian_Rhapsody "Bohemian Rhapsody")" on the BBC music series *[Top of the Pops](/wiki/Top_of_the_Pops "Top of the Pops")*. According to rock historian Paul Fowles, the song is "widely credited as the first global hit single for which an accompanying video was central to the marketing strategy".[[39]](#cite_note-Fowles-39) *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* has said of "Bohemian Rhapsody": "Its influence cannot be overstated, practically inventing the music video seven [sic] years before MTV went on the air."[[40]](#cite_note-40)

At the end of the 1970s, the broadcasting of music videos on television became more and more regular, in several countries. The music videos were, for example, broadcast in weekly music programs or inserted into various programs. In the United States, for example, on terrestrial networks at the end of the 1970s, music videos were sometimes broadcast on music shows: *[The Midnight Special](/wiki/The_Midnight_Special_(TV_series) "The Midnight Special (TV series)")*, *[Don Kirshner's Rock Concert](/wiki/Don_Kirshner%27s_Rock_Concert "Don Kirshner's Rock Concert")*, and occasionally on certain talk shows.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

A worldwide pioneer in programs that only transmitted rock and pop music video clips was the Peruvian program Disco Club, hosted by the Peruvian musician Gerardo Manuel, which began its transmission on the Peruvian state channel (Channel 7 of [Lima](/wiki/Lima "Lima"), Peru in free-to-air TV) in June 1978, three years before the appearance of MTV. Initially, it was only broadcast on Saturdays at 7 p.m. , but due to acceptance, in November of that same year it began to be broadcast every day.

*[Video Concert Hall](/wiki/Video_Concert_Hall "Video Concert Hall")*, created by Jerry Crowe and [Charles Henderson](/wiki/C.W._Henderson "C.W. Henderson") and launched on November 1, 1979, was the first nationwide video music programming on American cable television, predating MTV by almost two years.[[42]](#cite_note-ATL_firm-42)[[43]](#cite_note-King-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Werts-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Denisoff-45) The [USA Cable Network](/wiki/USA_Network "USA Network") program *[Night Flight](/wiki/Night_Flight_(TV_series) "Night Flight (TV series)")* was one of the first American programs to showcase these videos as an art form.

In 1980, the music video to [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie")'s "[Ashes to Ashes](/wiki/Ashes_to_Ashes_(David_Bowie_song) "Ashes to Ashes (David Bowie song)")" became the [most expensive ever made](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_music_videos "List of most expensive music videos"), having a production cost of $582,000 (equivalent to $2.27 million in 2025), the first music video to have a production cost of over $500,000.[[46]](#cite_note-Strange_Fascination-46) The video was made in [solarized](/wiki/Solarization_(photography) "Solarization (photography)") color with stark black-and-white scenes and was filmed in different locations, including a padded room and a rocky shore.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The video became one of the most iconic ever made at the time, and its complex nature is seen as significant in the evolution of the music video.

The same year, New Zealander group [Split Enz](/wiki/Split_Enz "Split Enz") had major success with the single "[I Got You](/wiki/I_Got_You_(Split_Enz_song) "I Got You (Split Enz song)")" and the album *[True Colours](/wiki/True_Colours_(Split_Enz_album) "True Colours (Split Enz album)")*, and later that year they produced a complete set of promo clips for each song on the album (directed by their percussionist, [Noel Crombie](/wiki/Noel_Crombie "Noel Crombie")) and to market these on videocassette. This was followed a year later by the video album, *[The Completion Backward Principle](/wiki/The_Completion_Backward_Principle "The Completion Backward Principle")* by [The Tubes](/wiki/The_Tubes "The Tubes"), directed by the group's keyboard player, Michael Cotten, which included two videos directed by Russell Mulcahy ("Talk to Ya Later" and "Don't Want to Wait Anymore").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Among the first music videos were clips produced by [ex-Monkee](/wiki/The_Monkees "The Monkees") [Michael Nesmith](/wiki/Michael_Nesmith "Michael Nesmith"), who started making short musical films for *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")*.[[25]](#cite_note-TMT-25) In 1981, he released *[Elephant Parts](/wiki/Elephant_Parts "Elephant Parts")*, the first winner of a [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy "Grammy") for music video, directed by William Dear.

### 1981–1991: Music videos go mainstream

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1981–1991: Music videos go mainstream")]

In 1981, the American video channel [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") launched, airing "[Video Killed the Radio Star](/wiki/Video_Killed_the_Radio_Star "Video Killed the Radio Star")" by [The Buggles](/wiki/The_Buggles "The Buggles") and beginning an era of 24/7 music on television. With this new outlet for material, the music video would, by the mid-1980s, grow to play a central role in popular music marketing. Many important acts of this period, most notably [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson"), [Adam and the Ants](/wiki/Adam_and_the_Ants "Adam and the Ants"), [Duran Duran](/wiki/Duran_Duran "Duran Duran") and [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna"), owed a great deal of their success to the skillful construction and seductive appeal of their videos.

Two key innovations in the development of the modern music video were the development of relatively inexpensive and easy-to-use [video](/wiki/Video "Video") recording and editing equipment, and the development of visual effects created with techniques such as image [compositing](/wiki/Compositing "Compositing").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The advent of high-quality color videotape recorders and portable video cameras coincided with the DIY ethos of the [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music") era,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] enabling many pop acts to produce promotional videos quickly and cheaply, in comparison to the relatively high costs of using film. However, as the genre developed, [music video directors](/wiki/Music_video_director "Music video director") increasingly turned to 35mm film as the preferred medium, while others mixed film and video.
During the 1980s, music videos had become *de rigueur* for most recording artists. The phenomenon was famously parodied by [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") television comedy program *[Not The Nine O'Clock News](/wiki/Not_The_Nine_O%27Clock_News "Not The Nine O'Clock News")* who produced a spoof music video "Nice Video, Shame About the Song" (the title was a spoof of a recent pop hit "[Nice Legs, Shame About Her Face](/wiki/Bad_Habits_(The_Monks_album) "Bad Habits (The Monks album)")").

In this period, directors and the acts they worked with began to discover and expand the form and style of the genre, using more sophisticated effects in their videos, mixing film and video, and adding a storyline or plot to the music video. Occasionally videos were made in a *non-representational* form, in which the musical artist was not shown. Because music videos are mainly intended to promote the artist, such videos are comparatively rare; three early 1980s examples are [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen")'s "[Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City_(song) "Atlantic City (song)")", directed by Arnold Levine, [David Mallet](/wiki/David_Mallet_(director) "David Mallet (director)")'s video for [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie") and [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)")'s "[Under Pressure](/wiki/Under_Pressure "Under Pressure")", and [Ian Emes](/wiki/Ian_Emes "Ian Emes")' video for [Duran Duran](/wiki/Duran_Duran "Duran Duran")'s "[The Chauffeur](/wiki/The_Chauffeur "The Chauffeur")". One notable later example of the non-representational style is Bill Konersman's innovative 1987 video for [Prince](/wiki/Prince_(musician) "Prince (musician)")'s "[Sign o' the Times](/wiki/Sign_o%27_the_Times_(song) "Sign o' the Times (song)")"[[49]](#cite_note-49) – influenced by Dylan's "Subterranean Homesick Blues" clip, it featured only the text of the song's lyrics.

In the early 1980s, music videos also began to discover political and social themes. Examples include the music videos for [David Bowie's](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie") "[China Girl](/wiki/China_Girl_(song) "China Girl (song)")" and "[Let's Dance](/wiki/Let%27s_Dance_(David_Bowie_song) "Let's Dance (David Bowie song)")" (1983) which both discussed race issues.[[50]](#cite_note-50) In a 1983 interview, Bowie spoke about the importance of using music videos in addressing social issues, "Let's try to use the video format as a platform for some kind of social observation, and not just waste it on trotting out and trying to enhance the public image of the singer involved".[[51]](#cite_note-51)

In 1983, one of the most successful, influential and iconic music videos of all time was released: the nearly 14-minute-long video for [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson")'s song "[Thriller](/wiki/Michael_Jackson%27s_Thriller_(music_video) "Michael Jackson's Thriller (music video)")", directed by [John Landis](/wiki/John_Landis "John Landis"). The video set new standards for production, having cost US $800,000 to film.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) The video for "Thriller", along with earlier videos by Jackson for his songs "[Billie Jean](/wiki/Billie_Jean "Billie Jean")" and "[Beat It](/wiki/Beat_It "Beat It")", were instrumental in getting music videos by [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American") artists played on MTV. Prior to Jackson's success, videos by African-American artists were rarely played on MTV: according to MTV, this was because it initially conceived itself as a rock-music-oriented channel, although musician [Rick James](/wiki/Rick_James "Rick James") was outspoken in his criticism of the cable channel, claiming in 1983 that MTV's refusal to air the music video for his song "[Super Freak](/wiki/Super_Freak "Super Freak")" and clips by other African-American performers was "blatant racism".[[54]](#cite_note-54) British rock singer David Bowie had also recently lashed out against MTV during an interview that he did with them prior to the release of "Thriller", stating that he was "floored" by how much MTV neglected black artists, bringing attention to how videos by the "few black artists that one does see" only appeared on MTV between 2:00 a.m. until 6:00 a.m. when nobody was watching.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

MTV also influences music video shows aired on other American TV channels, such as: *[Friday Night Videos](/wiki/Friday_Night_Videos "Friday Night Videos")*, launched in 1983 on the terrestrial network NBC and *MV3* launched in 1982.

On March 5, 1983, [Country Music Television](/wiki/Country_Music_Television "Country Music Television") (CMT), was launched,[[56]](#cite_note-56) created and founded by Glenn D. Daniels and uplinked from the Video World Productions facility in [Hendersonville, Tennessee](/wiki/Hendersonville,_Tennessee "Hendersonville, Tennessee"). The [MuchMusic](/wiki/MuchMusic "MuchMusic") video channel was launched in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") in 1984. In 1984, MTV also launched the [MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Awards "MTV Video Music Awards") (later to be known as the VMAs), an annual awards event that would come to underscore MTV's importance in the music industry. The inaugural event rewarded [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") and [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie") with the [Video Vanguard Award](/wiki/Michael_Jackson_Video_Vanguard_Award "Michael Jackson Video Vanguard Award") for their work in pioneering the music video.

In 1985, MTV's Viacom (currently Paramount) launched the channel [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1") (then known as "VH-1: Video Hits One"), featuring softer music, and meant to cater to the slightly older baby-boomer demographic who were out-growing MTV. Internationally, [MTV Europe](/wiki/MTV_Europe "MTV Europe") was launched in 1987, and [MTV Asia](/wiki/MTV_Asia "MTV Asia") in 1991. Another important development in music videos was the launch of *[The Chart Show](/wiki/The_Chart_Show "The Chart Show")* on the UK's [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4") in 1986. This was a program that composed entirely of music videos (the only outlet many videos had on British TV at the time[[57]](#cite_note-57)), with no presenters. Instead, the videos were linked by then state of the art [computer graphics](/wiki/Computer_graphics "Computer graphics"). The show moved to [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)") in 1989.

The video for the 1985 [Dire Straits](/wiki/Dire_Straits "Dire Straits") song "[Money for Nothing](/wiki/Money_for_Nothing_(song) "Money for Nothing (song)")" made pioneering use of computer animation, and helped make the song an international hit. The song itself was a wry comment on the music-video phenomenon, sung from the point of view of an appliance deliveryman both drawn to and repelled by the outlandish images and personalities that appeared on MTV. In 1986, [Peter Gabriel](/wiki/Peter_Gabriel "Peter Gabriel")'s song "[Sledgehammer](/wiki/Sledgehammer_(Peter_Gabriel_song) "Sledgehammer (Peter Gabriel song)")" used special effects and animation techniques developed by British studio [Aardman Animations](/wiki/Aardman_Animations "Aardman Animations"). The video for "Sledgehammer" would go on to be a phenomenal success[[58]](#cite_note-58) and win nine MTV Video Music Awards. In the same year, [Kraftwerk](/wiki/Kraftwerk "Kraftwerk") released the song "[Musique Non Stop](/wiki/Musique_Non_Stop "Musique Non Stop")". The video featured 3D animations of the group. It was a collaboration with [Rebecca Allen](/wiki/Rebecca_Allen_(artist) "Rebecca Allen (artist)") of the [New York Institute of Technology](/wiki/New_York_Institute_of_Technology "New York Institute of Technology") and ran continuously on [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") for a while.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In 1988, the show *[Yo! MTV Raps](/wiki/Yo!_MTV_Raps "Yo! MTV Raps")* introduced; the show helped to bring [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") to a mass audience for the first time.

### 1992–2004: Rise of the directors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1992–2004: Rise of the directors")]

In November 1992, [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") began screening videos made by [Chris Cunningham](/wiki/Chris_Cunningham "Chris Cunningham"), [Michel Gondry](/wiki/Michel_Gondry "Michel Gondry"), [Spike Jonze](/wiki/Spike_Jonze "Spike Jonze"), [Floria Sigismondi](/wiki/Floria_Sigismondi "Floria Sigismondi"),[[60]](#cite_note-60) [Stéphane Sednaoui](/wiki/St%C3%A9phane_Sednaoui "Stéphane Sednaoui"), [Mark Romanek](/wiki/Mark_Romanek "Mark Romanek") and [Hype Williams](/wiki/Hype_Williams "Hype Williams") who all got their start around this time; all brought a unique vision and style to the videos they directed. Some of these directors, including, Gondry, Jonze, Sigismondi,[[61]](#cite_note-61) and [F. Gary Gray](/wiki/F._Gary_Gray "F. Gary Gray"), went on to direct feature films. This continued a trend that had begun earlier with directors such as [Lasse Hallström](/wiki/Lasse_Hallstr%C3%B6m "Lasse Hallström") and [David Fincher](/wiki/David_Fincher "David Fincher").

Two of the videos directed by Romanek in 1995 are notable for being two of the three [most expensive music videos of all time](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_music_videos "List of most expensive music videos"): [Michael](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson") and [Janet Jackson](/wiki/Janet_Jackson "Janet Jackson")'s "[Scream](/wiki/Scream_(Michael_Jackson_and_Janet_Jackson_song) "Scream (Michael Jackson and Janet Jackson song)")", which allegedly cost $7 million to produce, and [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna")'s "[Bedtime Story](/wiki/Bedtime_Story_(Madonna_song) "Bedtime Story (Madonna song)")", which cost a reported $5 million. From this, "Scream" is the most expensive video to date. In the mid to late 1990s, [Walter Stern](/wiki/Walter_Stern_(director) "Walter Stern (director)") directed "[Firestarter](/wiki/Firestarter_(The_Prodigy_song) "Firestarter (The Prodigy song)")" by [The Prodigy](/wiki/The_Prodigy "The Prodigy"), "[Bitter Sweet Symphony](/wiki/Bitter_Sweet_Symphony "Bitter Sweet Symphony")" by [The Verve](/wiki/The_Verve "The Verve"), and "[Teardrop](/wiki/Teardrop_(Massive_Attack_song) "Teardrop (Massive Attack song)")" by [Massive Attack](/wiki/Massive_Attack "Massive Attack").[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

During this period, MTV launched channels around the world to show music videos produced in each local market: [MTV Latin America](/wiki/MTV_Latin_America "MTV Latin America") in 1993, [MTV India](/wiki/MTV_India "MTV India") in 1996, and [MTV Mandarin](/wiki/MTV_Mandarin "MTV Mandarin") in 1997, among others. [MTV2](/wiki/MTV2 "MTV2"), originally called "M2" and meant to show more alternative and older music videos, debuted in 1996.

In 1999, [Mariah Carey](/wiki/Mariah_Carey "Mariah Carey")'s "[Heartbreaker](/wiki/Heartbreaker_(Mariah_Carey_song) "Heartbreaker (Mariah Carey song)")" (featuring guest rapper [Jay-Z](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z")) became one of the [most expensive ever made](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_music_videos "List of most expensive music videos"), costing over $2.5 million.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

From 1991 to 2001, *Billboard* had its own Music Video Awards.

### 2005–present: Music video downloads and streaming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2005–present: Music video downloads and streaming")]

A video promoting Spoon's album *Spacey Boy and Sadness Girl*

The website [iFilm](/wiki/IFilm "IFilm"), which hosted short videos including music videos, launched in 1997. [Napster](/wiki/Napster "Napster"), a [peer-to-peer file sharing](/wiki/Peer-to-peer_file_sharing "Peer-to-peer file sharing") service which ran between 1999 and 2001, enabled users to share video files, including those for music videos. By the mid-2000s, MTV and many of its sister channels had largely abandoned showing music videos in favor of [reality](/wiki/Reality_television "Reality television") TV shows, which were more popular with its audiences, and which MTV had itself helped to pioneer with the show *[The Real World](/wiki/The_Real_World_(TV_series) "The Real World (TV series)")*, which premiered in 1992.

2005 saw the launch of [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"), which made the viewing of online video much faster and easier; [Google Videos](/wiki/Google_Videos "Google Videos"), [Yahoo! Video](/wiki/Yahoo!_Video "Yahoo! Video"), [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") and [Myspace](/wiki/Myspace "Myspace")'s video functionality use similar technology. Such websites had a profound effect on the viewing of music videos; some artists began to see success as a result of videos seen mostly or entirely online. The band [OK Go](/wiki/OK_Go "OK Go") capitalized on the growing trend, having achieved fame through the videos for two of their songs, "[A Million Ways](/wiki/A_Million_Ways "A Million Ways")" in 2005 and "[Here It Goes Again](/wiki/Here_It_Goes_Again "Here It Goes Again")" in 2006, both of which first became well-known online (OK Go repeated the trick with another high-concept video in 2010, for their song "[This Too Shall Pass](/wiki/This_Too_Shall_Pass_(OK_Go_song) "This Too Shall Pass (OK Go song)")").

At its launch, [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Computer "Apple Computer")'s [iTunes Store](/wiki/ITunes_Store "ITunes Store") provided a section of free music videos in high quality compression to be watched via the iTunes application. More recently the iTunes Store has begun selling music videos for use on Apple's [iPod](/wiki/IPod "IPod") with video playback capability.

The 2008 video for [Weezer](/wiki/Weezer "Weezer")'s "[Pork and Beans](/wiki/Pork_and_Beans_(song) "Pork and Beans (song)")" also captured this trend, by including at least 20 [YouTube celebrities](/wiki/YouTube_celebrities "YouTube celebrities"); the single became the most successful of Weezer's career, in chart performance. In 2007, the [RIAA](/wiki/RIAA "RIAA") issued cease-and-desist letters to YouTube users to prevent single users from sharing videos, which are the property of the music labels. After its merger with [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), YouTube assured the RIAA that they would find a way to pay [royalties](/wiki/Royalties "Royalties") through a bulk agreement with the major record labels.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This was complicated by the fact that not all labels share the same policy toward music videos: some welcome the development and upload music videos to various online outlets themselves, viewing music videos as free [advertising](/wiki/Advertising "Advertising") for their artists, while other labels view music videos not as an advertisement, but as the product itself.

To further signify the change in direction towards Music Video airplay, MTV officially dropped the Music Television tagline on February 8, 2010 from their logo in response to their increased commitment to non-scripted reality programming and other youth-oriented entertainment rising in prominence on their live broadcast.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

[Vevo](/wiki/Vevo "Vevo"), a music video service launched by several major music publishers, debuted in December 2009.[[66]](#cite_note-launch-66) The videos on Vevo are syndicated to YouTube, with [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") and Vevo sharing the advertising revenue.[[67]](#cite_note-cnetold-67)

As of 2017, the most-watched English-language video on YouTube was "[Shape of You](/wiki/Shape_of_You "Shape of You")" by [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran"). As of 2018, the most-watched remix video on YouTube was "[Te Bote](/wiki/Te_Bote "Te Bote")" by Casper Mágico featuring Nio García, [Darell](/wiki/Darell_(rapper) "Darell (rapper)"), [Nicky Jam](/wiki/Nicky_Jam "Nicky Jam"), [Bad Bunny](/wiki/Bad_Bunny "Bad Bunny"), and [Ozuna](/wiki/Ozuna_(singer) "Ozuna (singer)").

#### Official lo-fi Internet music clips

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Official lo-fi Internet music clips")]

Following the shift toward internet broadcasting and the rising popularity of user-generated video sites such as [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") around 2006, various [independent filmmakers](/wiki/Independent_filmmakers "Independent filmmakers") began films recording live sessions to present on the Web. All of these swiftly recorded clips are made with minimal budgets and share similar aesthetics with the [lo-fi music](/wiki/Lo-fi_music "Lo-fi music") movement of the early nineties. Offering freedom from the increasingly burdensome financial requirements of high-production movie-like clips, it began as the only method for little-known [indie music](/wiki/Indie_music "Indie music") artists to present themselves to a wider audience, but increasingly this approach has been taken up by such major mainstream artists as [R.E.M.](/wiki/R.E.M. "R.E.M.") and [Tom Jones](/wiki/Tom_Jones_(singer) "Tom Jones (singer)").[[68]](#cite_note-68)

#### Vertical videos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Vertical videos")]

In the late 2010s, some artists began releasing alternative [vertical videos](/wiki/Vertical_videos "Vertical videos") tailored to mobile devices in addition to music videos; these vertical videos are generally platform-exclusive.[[69]](#cite_note-69) These vertical videos are often shown on [Snapchat](/wiki/Snapchat "Snapchat")'s "Discover" section or within [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") playlists.[[70]](#cite_note-70) Early adopters of vertical video releases include the number-one hit "[Girls Like You](/wiki/Girls_Like_You "Girls Like You")" by [Maroon 5](/wiki/Maroon_5 "Maroon 5") featuring [Cardi B](/wiki/Cardi_B "Cardi B"). "[Idontwannabeyouanymore](/wiki/Idontwannabeyouanymore "Idontwannabeyouanymore")" by [Billie Eilish](/wiki/Billie_Eilish "Billie Eilish") is the most-watched vertical video on YouTube, although the presence of borders in the video actually make it in landscape.

#### Lyric videos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Lyric videos")]

A **lyric video** is a type of music video in which the [lyrics](/wiki/Lyrics "Lyrics") to the song are the primary visual element of the video. As such, they can be created with relative ease and often serve as a supplemental video to a more traditional music video.

The music video for [R.E.M.](/wiki/R.E.M. "R.E.M.")'s 1986 song "[Fall on Me](/wiki/Fall_on_Me_(R.E.M._song) "Fall on Me (R.E.M. song)")" interspersed the song's lyrics with abstract film footage. In 1987, [Prince](/wiki/Prince_(musician) "Prince (musician)") released a video for his song "[Sign o' the Times](/wiki/Sign_o%27_the_Times_(song) "Sign o' the Times (song)")". The video featured the song's words pulsing to the music, presented alongside abstract geometric shapes, an effect created by Bill Konersman.[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72) The following year, the video for the [Talking Heads](/wiki/Talking_Heads "Talking Heads") single "[(Nothing But) Flowers](/wiki/(Nothing_But)_Flowers "(Nothing But) Flowers")" composed of the song's lyrics superimposed onto or next to members of the band, was released. In 1990, [George Michael](/wiki/George_Michael "George Michael") released "[Praying for Time](/wiki/Praying_for_Time "Praying for Time")" as a lyric video. He had refused to make a traditional music video, so his label released a simple clip that displayed the song's lyrics on a black screen.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Lyric videos rose to greater prominence in the 2010s, when it became relatively easy for artists to disperse videos through websites such as [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube").[[74]](#cite_note-ew1-74) Many do not feature any visual related to the musician in question, but merely a background with the lyrics appearing over it as they are sung in the song.[[74]](#cite_note-ew1-74) In 2011, death metal band Krokmitën released the first lyric video for an entire album, "Alpha-Beta".[[75]](#cite_note-75) The concept album video featured imagery pulsing to the music and stylized typography created by bandleader Simlev. As of 2017, the 2016 song "[Closer](/wiki/Closer_(Chainsmokers_song)#Lyric_video "Closer (Chainsmokers song)")" by [The Chainsmokers](/wiki/The_Chainsmokers "The Chainsmokers"), featuring vocalist [Halsey](/wiki/Halsey_(singer) "Halsey (singer)"), is the most-watched lyric video on YouTube.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

## Censorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Censorship")]

See also: [Sexuality in music videos](/wiki/Sexuality_in_music_videos "Sexuality in music videos")

As the concept and medium of a music video is a form of artistic expression, artists have been on many occasions censored if their content is deemed offensive. What may be considered offensive will differ in countries due to censorship laws and local customs and ethics. In most cases, the record label will provide and distribute videos edited or provide both censored and uncensored videos for an artist. In some cases, it has been known for music videos to be banned in their entirety as they have been deemed far too offensive to be broadcast.

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 1980s")]

The first video to be banned by [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") was [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)")'s 1982 hit "[Body Language](/wiki/Body_Language_(Queen_song) "Body Language (Queen song)")". Due to thinly veiled homoerotic undertones plus much skin and sweat (but apparently not enough clothing, save that worn by the fully clothed members of Queen themselves), it was deemed unsuitable for a television audience at the time. However, the channel did air [Olivia Newton-John](/wiki/Olivia_Newton-John "Olivia Newton-John")'s 1981 video for the hit song "[Physical](/wiki/Physical_(Olivia_Newton-John_song) "Physical (Olivia Newton-John song)")", which lavished camera time on male models working out in string bikinis who spurn her advances, ultimately pairing off to walk to the men's locker rooms holding hands, though the network ended the clip before the overt homosexual "reveal" ending in some airings. The video for "[Girls on Film](/wiki/Girls_on_Film "Girls on Film")" by [Duran Duran](/wiki/Duran_Duran "Duran Duran"), which featured topless women mud wrestling and other depictions of sexual fetishes was banned by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"). MTV did air the video, albeit in a heavily edited form.

[Laura Branigan](/wiki/Laura_Branigan "Laura Branigan") initially protested an MTV request to edit her "[Self Control](/wiki/Self_Control_(Raf_song)#Laura_Branigan_version "Self Control (Raf song)")" video in 1984, but relented when the network refused to air the [William Friedkin](/wiki/William_Friedkin "William Friedkin")-directed clip, featuring the singer lured through an increasingly debauched, if increasingly stylized, series of nightclubs by a masked man who ultimately takes her to bed. In 1989, [Cher](/wiki/Cher "Cher")'s "[If I Could Turn Back Time](/wiki/If_I_Could_Turn_Back_Time "If I Could Turn Back Time")" video (where the singer performs the song in an extremely revealing body suit surrounded by a ship full of cheering sailors) was restricted to late-night broadcasts on MTV. [The Sex Pistols](/wiki/The_Sex_Pistols "The Sex Pistols")' video for "[God Save the Queen](/wiki/God_Save_the_Queen_(Sex_Pistols_song) "God Save the Queen (Sex Pistols song)")" was banned by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") for being "in gross bad taste". [Mötley Crüe](/wiki/M%C3%B6tley_Cr%C3%BCe "Mötley Crüe")'s video for "[Girls, Girls, Girls](/wiki/Girls,_Girls,_Girls_(M%C3%B6tley_Cr%C3%BCe_song) "Girls, Girls, Girls (Mötley Crüe song)")" was banned by MTV for having completely nude women dancing around the members of the band in a [strip club](/wiki/Strip_club "Strip club"), although they did produce another version that was accepted by MTV.

In 1983, *[Entertainment Tonight](/wiki/Entertainment_Tonight "Entertainment Tonight")* ran a segment on censorship and "Rock Video Violence". The episode explored the impact of [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") rock video violence on the youth of the early 1980s. Excerpts from the music videos of [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson"), [Duran Duran](/wiki/Duran_Duran "Duran Duran"), [Golden Earring](/wiki/Golden_Earring "Golden Earring"), [Kiss](/wiki/Kiss_(band) "Kiss (band)"), [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_(band) "Kansas (band)"), [Billy Idol](/wiki/Billy_Idol "Billy Idol"), [Def Leppard](/wiki/Def_Leppard "Def Leppard"), [Pat Benatar](/wiki/Pat_Benatar "Pat Benatar") and [The Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") were shown. Dr. [Thomas Radecki](/wiki/Thomas_Radecki "Thomas Radecki") of the National Coalition on TV Violence was interviewed accusing the fledgling rock video business of excessive violence. *[Night Tracks](/wiki/Night_Tracks "Night Tracks")* producer Tom Lynch weighed in on the effects of the video violence controversy. Recording artists [John Cougar Mellencamp](/wiki/John_Mellencamp "John Mellencamp"), [Gene Simmons](/wiki/Gene_Simmons "Gene Simmons") and [Paul Stanley](/wiki/Paul_Stanley "Paul Stanley") of Kiss, along with directors Dominic Orlando and [Julien Temple](/wiki/Julien_Temple "Julien Temple"), provided a defense of their work. The episode's conclusion was that the controversy will continue to grow. Some artists have used censorship as a publicity tool. In the 1980s, the show *Top of the Pops* was censorious in its approach to video content, so some acts made videos that they knew would be censored, using the resulting public controversy to promote their release. Examples of this tactic were [Duran Duran](/wiki/Duran_Duran "Duran Duran")'s aforementioned "Girls on Film" and [Frankie Goes to Hollywood](/wiki/Frankie_Goes_to_Hollywood "Frankie Goes to Hollywood") with "[Relax](/wiki/Relax_(Frankie_Goes_to_Hollywood_song) "Relax (Frankie Goes to Hollywood song)")", directed by [Bernard Rose](/wiki/Bernard_Rose_(director) "Bernard Rose (director)").

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1990s")]

In 1991, the dance segment of [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson")'s "[Black or White](/wiki/Black_or_White "Black or White")" was edited out because it showed Jackson "inappropriately" touching himself in it. His most controversial video, for "[They Don't Care About Us](/wiki/They_Don%27t_Care_About_Us "They Don't Care About Us")", was banned from MTV, VH1, and BBC due to the alleged [anti-Semitic](/wiki/Anti-Semitic "Anti-Semitic") messages in the song and the visuals in the background of the "Prison Version" of the video.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

[Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna") is the artist most associated with music video censorship. The controversy surrounding Madonna's marketing of her sexuality began with the video for "[Lucky Star](/wiki/Lucky_Star_(Madonna_song) "Lucky Star (Madonna song)")", and amplified over time due to clips such as "[Like a Virgin](/wiki/Like_a_Virgin_(song) "Like a Virgin (song)")". Outcry occurred over the subject matter (relating to [teenage pregnancy](/wiki/Teenage_pregnancy "Teenage pregnancy")) discussed in the video for the song "[Papa Don't Preach](/wiki/Papa_Don%27t_Preach "Papa Don't Preach")". "[Like a Prayer](/wiki/Like_a_Prayer_(song) "Like a Prayer (song)")" courted heavy criticism due to its religious, sexual, and racially oriented imagery. In 1990, Madonna's music video for the song "[Justify My Love](/wiki/Justify_My_Love "Justify My Love")" was banned by MTV due to its depiction of [sadomasochism](/wiki/Sadomasochism "Sadomasochism"), [homosexuality](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality"), [cross-dressing](/wiki/Cross-dressing "Cross-dressing"), and [group sex](/wiki/Group_sex "Group sex") which generated a media firestorm. In Canada, the debate over the banning of "Justify My Love" by the music video network [MuchMusic](/wiki/MuchMusic "MuchMusic") led to the launching in 1991 of *[Too Much 4 Much](/wiki/Too_Much_4_Much "Too Much 4 Much")*, a series of occasional, late-night specials (still being aired in the early 2000s) in which videos officially banned by MuchMusic were broadcast, followed by a panel discussion regarding why they were removed.

In 1992, [The Shamen](/wiki/The_Shamen "The Shamen")'s video for the song "[Ebeneezer Goode](/wiki/Ebeneezer_Goode "Ebeneezer Goode")" was banned by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") due to its perceived subliminal endorsement of the recreational drug [Ecstasy](/wiki/Ecstasy_(drug) "Ecstasy (drug)").[[78]](#cite_note-78) [The Prodigy](/wiki/The_Prodigy "The Prodigy")'s 1997 video for "[Smack My Bitch Up](/wiki/Smack_My_Bitch_Up "Smack My Bitch Up")" was banned in some countries due to depictions of drug use and nudity. The Prodigy's video for "[Firestarter](/wiki/Firestarter_(The_Prodigy_song) "Firestarter (The Prodigy song)")" was banned by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") due to its references to arson.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

In 1993, the Australian rock band [INXS](/wiki/INXS "INXS")' song "[The Gift](/wiki/The_Gift_(INXS_song) "The Gift (INXS song)")" was banned by MTV due to its use of Holocaust and Gulf War footage, among images of famine, pollution, war, and terrorism. As well as this, metal band [Tool](/wiki/Tool_(band) "Tool (band)")'s music video for "[Prison Sex](/wiki/Prison_Sex "Prison Sex")" was banned from MTV, as the video and lyrics touch on the sensitive matter of child abuse.

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2000s")]

In 2000, the music video for "[Rock DJ](/wiki/Rock_DJ "Rock DJ")" by [Robbie Williams](/wiki/Robbie_Williams "Robbie Williams") caused controversy due to the graphic nature of the video which features Williams stripping naked then peeling off his skin to reveal bloody flesh, followed by ripping off his muscles and organs until he is nothing but a blood-soaked skeleton. The video was censored in the UK during daytime hours and was broadcast unedited after 10 pm. The video was banned in [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic") due to allegations of [satanism](/wiki/Satanism "Satanism").[[80]](#cite_note-80)

In 2001, [Björk](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rk "Björk")'s video for "[Pagan Poetry](/wiki/Pagan_Poetry "Pagan Poetry")" was banned from MTV for depictions of sexual intercourse, fellatio, and body piercings. Her next single, "[Cocoon](/wiki/Cocoon_(Bj%C3%B6rk_song) "Cocoon (Björk song)")", was also banned by MTV as it featured a nude Björk (though the nude body was usually a fitted bodysuit rigged with red string).

In 2002, [t.A.T.u.](/wiki/T.A.T.u. "T.A.T.u.")'s video for "[All the Things She Said](/wiki/All_the_Things_She_Said "All the Things She Said")" caused controversy as it featured the young Russian girls, [Lena Katina](/wiki/Lena_Katina "Lena Katina") and [Yulia Volkova](/wiki/Yulia_Volkova "Yulia Volkova"), embracing and eventually kissing. British TV presenters [Richard and Judy](/wiki/Richard_and_Judy "Richard and Judy") campaigned to have the video banned claiming it pandered to "[pedophiles](/wiki/Pedophile "Pedophile")" with the use of school uniforms and young girls kissing, although the campaign failed. Capitalizing on the controversy, the kiss was choreographed into their live performances. *Top of the Pops* aired the girls' performance with the kiss replaced by audience footage. NBC's *The Tonight Show with Jay Leno* cut away from the girls' kiss to shots of the band. Throughout their promotional tour, t.A.T.u. protested by appearing in shirts reading "censored".

In 2004, [Maroon 5](/wiki/Maroon_5 "Maroon 5")'s video for "[This Love](/wiki/This_Love_(Maroon_5_song) "This Love (Maroon 5 song)")" generated controversy due to intimate scenes between the frontman [Adam Levine](/wiki/Adam_Levine "Adam Levine") and his then-girlfriend. Despite those particular scenes being shot at strategic angles, a censored version was released with a stream of [computer-generated](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") flowers added in to cover up more. The video for "[(s)AINT](/wiki/(s)AINT "(s)AINT")" by [Marilyn Manson](/wiki/Marilyn_Manson_(band) "Marilyn Manson (band)") was banned by their label due to its violence and sexual content. The following year, [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem")'s video for "[Just Lose It](/wiki/Just_Lose_It "Just Lose It")" caused controversy over its parody of Michael Jackson's [2005 child molestation trial](/wiki/People_v._Jackson "People v. Jackson"), [plastic surgery](/wiki/Health_and_appearance_of_Michael_Jackson "Health and appearance of Michael Jackson"), and hair catching fire during the filming of a [Pepsi](/wiki/Pepsi "Pepsi") commercial. The video was banned from [BET](/wiki/Black_Entertainment_Television "Black Entertainment Television"), and Jackson spoke out against the video, calling it "inappropriate and disrespectful to me, my children, my family, and the community at large". In 2004, many family groups and politicians lobbied for the banning of the [Eric Prydz](/wiki/Eric_Prydz "Eric Prydz") video "[Call on Me](/wiki/Call_on_Me_(Eric_Prydz_song) "Call on Me (Eric Prydz song)")" for containing women dancing in a sexually suggestive way; however, the video was not banned.

As of 2005, the Egyptian state censorship committee banned at least 20 music videos which featured sexual connotations due to Muslim moral viewpoints.[[81]](#cite_note-81) The music video of "[These Boots Are Made for Walkin'](/wiki/These_Boots_Are_Made_for_Walkin%27#Jessica_Simpson_version "These Boots Are Made for Walkin'")" which featured [Jessica Simpson](/wiki/Jessica_Simpson "Jessica Simpson") in character as Daisy Duke, was controversial for featuring Simpson in "revealing" outfits and washing the [General Lee car](/wiki/General_Lee_(car) "General Lee (car)") in her bikini.[[82]](#cite_note-82) The controversy resulted in the music video being banned in some countries.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

In 2008, [Justice](/wiki/Justice_(French_band) "Justice (French band)")'s video for their song "Stress" was boycotted by several major music television channels due to allegations of racism and violence; the video depicts several youths committing different crimes throughout the streets of [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), with the youths mainly being of [North African](/wiki/North_African "North African") descent.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

While country music has largely avoided controversy surrounding video content, it has never been immune. The music video for the 2003 [Rascal Flatts](/wiki/Rascal_Flatts "Rascal Flatts") song "[I Melt](/wiki/I_Melt "I Melt")" is a case in point, gaining notoriety for clips featuring guitarist [Joe Don Rooney](/wiki/Joe_Don_Rooney "Joe Don Rooney")'s bare buttocks, and model Christina Auria taking a shower nude.[[85]](#cite_note-cmt-85) The video was the first aired on CMT to show nudity,[[86]](#cite_note-contact-86) and eventually reached #1 on the network's "Top Twenty Countdown" program.[[87]](#cite_note-87) However, [GAC](/wiki/Great_American_Country "Great American Country") banned the video when the group refused to release an edited version.[[86]](#cite_note-contact-86)

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 2010s")]

In 2010, [Thirty Seconds to Mars](/wiki/Thirty_Seconds_to_Mars "Thirty Seconds to Mars")' video "[Hurricane](/wiki/Hurricane_(Thirty_Seconds_to_Mars_song) "Hurricane (Thirty Seconds to Mars song)")" was censored due to its major elements of violence, nudity and sex.[[88]](#cite_note-88) The short film was later released with a clean version that can air on television.[[89]](#cite_note-89) The explicit version is available on the band's official website with a viewing certificate of 18+.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

In 2010, a rumor circulated that [Lady Gaga](/wiki/Lady_Gaga "Lady Gaga")'s video "[Telephone](/wiki/Telephone_(video) "Telephone (video)")" was banned by MTV, a rumour which reached some press outlets. The rumor claimed that MTV had banned the video because the content could not be shown within their programming. MTV denied the ban and showed the video frequently on European MTV programming.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Lady Gaga's previous videos have also attracted criticism for their sexually suggestive content; the video for "[LoveGame](/wiki/LoveGame "LoveGame")" was not played on the Australian music video program *[Video Hits](/wiki/Video_Hits_(Australian_TV_series) "Video Hits (Australian TV series)")*; however, other Australian programs aired the video uncensored. The video for "[Alejandro](/wiki/Alejandro_(Lady_Gaga_song) "Alejandro (Lady Gaga song)")" was criticized by the [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(U.S.) "Catholic League (U.S.)"), for showing the singer dressed in a red [latex fetish](/wiki/Latex_and_PVC_fetishism "Latex and PVC fetishism") version of a [nun's habit](/wiki/Nun%27s_habit "Nun's habit"), simulating rape, and appearing to swallow a [rosary](/wiki/Rosary "Rosary").[[92]](#cite_note-92)

[Ciara](/wiki/Ciara "Ciara")'s video for "[Ride](/wiki/Ride_(Ciara_song) "Ride (Ciara song)")" was banned by [BET](/wiki/Black_Entertainment_Television "Black Entertainment Television"), with the network citing that the video was too sexually charged. The video was also subsequently banned by all UK television channels.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

In 2011, the video for "[S&M](/wiki/S%26M_(song) "S&M (song)")", which features the Barbadian singer [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") whipping a tied-up white man, taking hostages and indulging in a lesbian kiss, was banned in eleven countries and was flagged as inappropriate for viewers that are under 18 on YouTube.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2020s")]

In 2019, [Lil Nas X](/wiki/Lil_Nas_X "Lil Nas X")'s viral song "Old Town Road" became the longest-running number-one song on the Billboard Hot 100 chart. However, it was his follow-up song named "Montero (Call Me By Your Name)" which was released in 2021 that raised the controversy. In the music video for "[Montero](/wiki/Montero_(Call_Me_by_Your_Name) "Montero (Call Me by Your Name)")", Lil Nas X included various provocative scenes, including one in which he gives a lap dance to Satan. The video was widely criticized by conservative and religious groups, who saw it as promoting Satanism and immorality. Lil Nas X responded to the backlash by defending that it was just a way of expressing his own sexuality and challenging societal norms. He profited from the controversy by promoting his own merchandise, including a pair of "Satan Shoes" that were made in collaboration with a company called MSCHF, which featured a bronze pentagram, an inverted cross and a drop of real human blood.[[95]](#cite_note-95) The controversy related to "Montero" eventually helped dragging the song to even greater success, debuting at number one on the Billboard Hot 100 and earning applause from critics for its bold and innovative approach to music and visuals.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

In 2023, the music video for "[I'm Not Here to Make Friends](/wiki/I%27m_Not_Here_to_Make_Friends "I'm Not Here to Make Friends")" by [Sam Smith](/wiki/Sam_Smith "Sam Smith") drew criticism and sparked debate over whether it should be censored. Critics claimed that the music video was sexualised, irresponsible, improper, and not good for society, where as the discussion on [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)")'s *[Good Morning Britain](/wiki/Good_Morning_Britain_(2014_TV_programme) "Good Morning Britain (2014 TV programme)")* inquired as to whether it was no different and similar to the Madonna music videos of the '80s and '90s. Proponents claimed that it was not different to Rocky Horror Picture Show, or the Frankie Goes to Hollywood's "Relax" music video that were successful decades ago.[[97]](#cite_note-97)
