In 2023, the music video "[Try That in a Small Town](/wiki/Try_That_in_a_Small_Town "Try That in a Small Town")" by [Jason Aldean](/wiki/Jason_Aldean "Jason Aldean") was banned by [CMT](/wiki/CMT_(American_TV_channel) "CMT (American TV channel)") due to controversy. Lyrics expressed behaviours that supposedly happen in the big cities that would be perceived to not be liked in a small town, such as, "carjack an old lady"; "cuss out a cop"; and "stomp on the flag." United States State Representative [Justin Jones](/wiki/Justin_Jones_(Tennessee_politician) "Justin Jones (Tennessee politician)") of Tennessee, a US Democrat, condemned the song on Twitter, describing it as a "heinous song calling for racist violence" that promoted "a shameful vision of gun extremism and vigilantism."

Aldean then defended himself on Twitter, asserting that he had been wrongly accused of releasing a pro-lynching song, and that he was "not too pleased" with the 2020 Black Lives Matter protests.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

In 2024, Spanish authorities announced an investigation into pop star Katy Perry following the filming of a music video on the islands of [Ibiza](/wiki/Ibiza "Ibiza") and [Formentera](/wiki/Formentera "Formentera"). The video, for her song LIFETIMES, featured Perry dancing along the beaches of the [Balearic Islands](/wiki/Balearic_Islands "Balearic Islands") and attending nightclubs.

According to a press release issued by local government authorities, some scenes had allegedly been filmed in areas where Perry and her production company had not obtained the necessary permits.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

Kim Kardashian's 2024 cover of the Christmas song Santa Baby, produced by her brother-in-law Travis Barker, attracted significant public attention for its unconventional music video. The video depicts Kardashian crawling on all fours through a surreal house party, encountering various unusual scenes, including a donkey, a man dressed as Jesus, and a businessman petting a reindeer mascot. The video's eccentric visuals prompted mixed reactions from viewers, with some describing it as "unhinged," "scary," and "demonic."[[100]](#cite_note-100)

## Commercial release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Commercial release")]

### Video album

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Video album")]

See also: [Visual album](/wiki/Visual_album "Visual album")

Music videos have been released commercially on physical formats such as [videotape](/wiki/Videotape "Videotape"), [LaserDisc](/wiki/LaserDisc "LaserDisc"), [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray"). Similar to an [audio album](/wiki/Album "Album"), a video album is a long-form release containing multiple music videos on a disc. The market for video albums is considerably smaller than for audio albums and [audio singles](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)"). Video albums are eligible for [gold certifications](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") from the [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") (RIAA) after record labels shipped 50,000 units to retailers, while both audio albums and singles have to ship 500,000 units to achieve gold.[[101]](#cite_note-RIAA-101) One of the early video albums was *[Eat to the Beat](/wiki/Eat_to_the_Beat#Video_album_(12"_Laser_Video_Format) "Eat to the Beat")* (1979) by American rock band [Blondie](/wiki/Blondie_(band) "Blondie (band)"), a [videocassette](/wiki/Videocassette "Videocassette") containing music videos of all tracks from their fourth studio album of the same name. It was produced by Paul Flattery for Jon Roseman Productions and directed by David Mallet. The music videos were recorded in New York and [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), with some songs featuring the band playing in a concert fashion, and some others having scenarios based on the songs' lyrics.[[102]](#cite_note-102) Another popular video album was *[Olivia Physical](/wiki/Olivia_Physical "Olivia Physical")* (1982) by [Olivia Newton-John](/wiki/Olivia_Newton-John "Olivia Newton-John"), which won the [Video of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Video_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Video of the Year") at the [25th Grammy Awards](/wiki/25th_Grammy_Awards "25th Grammy Awards").[[103]](#cite_note-103) The video collection features music videos of all songs from her ninth studio album, *[Physical](/wiki/Physical_(Olivia_Newton-John_album) "Physical (Olivia Newton-John album)")* (1981).

Due to the increase of video albums popularity, *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine introduced the weekly best-selling music video sales ranking in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), titled the [Top Music Videocassette](/wiki/Billboard_charts "Billboard charts") chart on March 30, 1985 (now known as Music Video Sales chart).[[104]](#cite_note-104) Its first chart-topper was *[Private Dancer](/wiki/Private_Dancer "Private Dancer")* (1984), a videocassette by [Tina Turner](/wiki/Tina_Turner "Tina Turner") containing four music videos.[[105]](#cite_note-105) The [Official Charts Company](/wiki/Official_Charts_Company "Official Charts Company") began the similar chart in the United Kingdom on January 30, 1994, with [Bryan Adams](/wiki/Bryan_Adams "Bryan Adams")'s *[So Far So Good](/wiki/So_Far_So_Good_(Bryan_Adams_album) "So Far So Good (Bryan Adams album)")* reaching number one.[[106]](#cite_note-106) According to the RIAA, the [Eagles](/wiki/Eagles_(band) "Eagles (band)")' *[Farewell 1 Tour-Live from Melbourne](/wiki/Farewell_1_Tour-Live_from_Melbourne "Farewell 1 Tour-Live from Melbourne")* (2005) is the top-certified longform music video with 30-time platinum (three million units shipped),[[107]](#cite_note-107) while [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones")' *[Four Flicks](/wiki/Four_Flicks "Four Flicks")* (2005) is the top-certified music video [boxset](/wiki/Boxset "Boxset") with 19-time platinum (1.9 million units shipped).[[108]](#cite_note-108) The term "visual album" achieved prominence in modern usage after the release of American singer-songwriter [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé")'s 2013 [self-titled album](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9_(album) "Beyoncé (album)").[[109]](#cite_note-piece-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110)

### Video single

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Video single")]

Main article: [Vingle](/wiki/Vingle "Vingle")

A video single contains no more than three music videos in the form of a videotape, LaserDisc or DVD. In 1983, British [synth-pop](/wiki/Synth-pop "Synth-pop") band [the Human League](/wiki/The_Human_League "The Human League") released the first commercial video single titled *[The Human League Video Single](/wiki/The_Human_League_Video_Single_(1983) "The Human League Video Single (1983)")* on both VHS and Betamax.[[111]](#cite_note-111) It was not a huge commercial success due to the high retail price of £10.99, compared to around £1.99 for a 7" vinyl single. The VHS single gained higher levels of mainstream popularity when [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna") released "[Justify My Love](/wiki/Justify_My_Love "Justify My Love")" as a video single in 1990 following the blacklisting of the video by [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV"). "Justify My Love" remains the best-selling video single of all time.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

The DVD single was introduced in the late 1990s as a replacement for the videotape single. Although many record companies in the United States refused to issue [CD singles](/wiki/CD_single "CD single"), they readily issued DVD singles, and some popular DVD singles include [Kelly Clarkson](/wiki/Kelly_Clarkson "Kelly Clarkson")'s "[A Moment Like This](/wiki/A_Moment_Like_This "A Moment Like This")", [Jessica Simpson](/wiki/Jessica_Simpson "Jessica Simpson")'s "[With You](/wiki/With_You_(Jessica_Simpson_song) "With You (Jessica Simpson song)")", [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé")'s "[Crazy in Love](/wiki/Crazy_in_Love_(Beyonc%C3%A9_Knowles_song) "Crazy in Love (Beyoncé Knowles song)")", [Christina Aguilera](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera "Christina Aguilera")'s "[Fighter](/wiki/Fighter_(Christina_Aguilera_song) "Fighter (Christina Aguilera song)")", [Britney Spears](/wiki/Britney_Spears "Britney Spears")'s "[Toxic](/wiki/Toxic_(song) "Toxic (song)")" and [Iron Maiden](/wiki/Iron_Maiden "Iron Maiden")'s "[Satellite 15... The Final Frontier](/wiki/Satellite_15..._The_Final_Frontier "Satellite 15... The Final Frontier")". According to the RIAA, a music video single is defined as 1-2 songs per video OR under 15 minutes running time. In 2003, the first certified platinum and gold music DVD singles were certified by the RIAA.[[113]](#cite_note-Wolf_2003-113) Noteworthy early DVD singles in the United States include [Sly and Robbie](/wiki/Sly_and_Robbie "Sly and Robbie")'s "Superthruster" (1999), [Björk](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rk "Björk")'s "[All Is Full of Love](/wiki/All_Is_Full_of_Love "All Is Full of Love")" (1999), and [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna")'s "[Music](/wiki/Music_(Madonna_song) "Music (Madonna song)")" (2000).[[114]](#cite_note-Mancini_2000-114)

In the United Kingdom where up to 3 physical formats are eligible for the [chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart"), DVD singles are quite common (with the single available on DVD as well as CD and/or [vinyl record](/wiki/Gramophone_records "Gramophone records")). As with other single formats, DVD singles have a limited production run, often causing them to become collector's items. The DVD single never experienced a high amount of popularity in the United Kingdom because when artists started releasing them in the early 2000s, the CD single had started declining. They were also seen as expensive. Some artists would not release DVD singles and instead put their music videos as enhanced content on a CD single/album.

Beginning in the early 2000s, artists in Japan may release singles in the CD+DVD format. Japanese singer [Ayumi Hamasaki](/wiki/Ayumi_Hamasaki "Ayumi Hamasaki") has been credited as the "creator of the CD+DVD format"; one of the examples is her 2005 single "[Fairyland](/wiki/Fairyland_(song) "Fairyland (song)")". The CD+DVD format is more expensive and usually contains one or more music videos, and sometimes a "making of" section or other bonus material is included.

The Japanese music conglomerate [Hello! Project](/wiki/Hello!_Project "Hello! Project") released corresponding DVD singles for almost all of its CD single releases. The company calls them Single Vs. A Single V usually contains a music video for the title song plus several more of its versions and a [making-of](/wiki/Making-of "Making-of"). Sometimes, an **Event V** (エベントV) will be released at Hello! Project [fan club](/wiki/Fan_club "Fan club") events that will offer alternate shots of a promotional video, or bonus footage, like backstage footage or footage from a photoshoot not released anywhere else. As of 2017, Single Vs were no longer released; instead Hello! Project acts now put the music videos on DVDs included in a CD single's limited edition. The DVD singles are popular and chart in the generic [Oricon](/wiki/Oricon "Oricon") DVD sales chart, due to the non-existence of a separate DVD single ranking in Japan.

## Unofficial music videos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_video&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Unofficial music videos")]

Unofficial, fan-made music videos are typically made by synchronizing existing footage from other sources, such as television series or films, with the song. The first known fan video, or [songvid](/wiki/Vidding "Vidding"), was created by [Kandy Fong](/wiki/Kandy_Fong "Kandy Fong") in 1975 using still images from *Star Trek* loaded into a slide carousel and played in conjunction with a song.[[115]](#cite_note-115) Fan videos made using [videocassette recorders](/wiki/Videocassette_recorder "Videocassette recorder") soon followed.[[116]](#cite_note-116) With the advent of easy distribution over the internet and cheap video-editing software, fan-created videos began to gain wider notice in the late 1990s.

A well-known example of an unofficial video is one made for [Danger Mouse](/wiki/Danger_Mouse_(music_producer) "Danger Mouse (music producer)")'s illegal [mashup](/wiki/Mashup_(music) "Mashup (music)") from his *[The Grey Album](/wiki/The_Grey_Album "The Grey Album")*, of the [Jay-Z](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z") track "[Encore](/wiki/Encore_(Jay-Z_song) "Encore (Jay-Z song)")" with music sampled from [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles")' *[White Album](/wiki/The_Beatles_(album) "The Beatles (album)")*, in which concert footage of the Beatles is remixed with footage of [Jay-Z](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z") and hip-hop dancers.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

In 2004, a [Placebo](/wiki/Placebo_(band) "Placebo (band)") fan from South Africa[[118]](#cite_note-118) made a [claymation](/wiki/Claymation "Claymation") video for the band's song "[English Summer Rain](/wiki/English_Summer_Rain "English Summer Rain")" and sent it to the band. They liked the result so much that it was included on their greatest hits DVD.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

In 2016, a [Flash animation](/wiki/Flash_animation "Flash animation") for the song "[Come Together](/wiki/Come_Together "Come Together")" by the Beatles was included on The Beatles [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") disc.
